name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (١)
```

```english
In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢)
```

```english
All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds, (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٣)
```

```english
the All-beneficent, the All-merciful, (3)
```

```arabic
مَـٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤)
```

```english
Master of the Day of Retribution. (4)
```

```arabic
إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (٥)
```

```english
You [alone] do we worship, and to You [alone] do we turn for help. (5)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم (In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful). People often perform an act or begin an undertaking and couple it with the name of one of their dear ones or one of their great figures, so that their act may be blessed by that name and honored by it, or so that it may serve as a memorial by which they remember him. Similar to this is the practice in the domain of naming; they often name a newborn child, or something they have manufactured or performed—such as a house they built or an institution they established—after someone they love or revere, so that the name remains through the persistence of the newly named object, and the original person named retains a sort of persistence through the persistence of the name, like one who names his son after his father to keep his memory alive so that it does not perish or fade away.

The speech of the Exalted has followed this course, so He began the speech with His name, exalted is His Name (عز إسمه); so that what it contains of meaning may be marked by His name and linked to Him, and so that it may be an etiquette (أدب) by which the servants are disciplined in their acts, deeds, and words, such that they begin with His name and act by it. Thus, what they do becomes marked by His name, characterized by His attribute, the Exalted, and intended for His sake, the Glorified; consequently, the act will not be perishing, vain, or severed (مبتراً), because it is in the name of Allah, to Whom there is no path for perishing or vanity.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, explains in various places of His speech: that whatever is not for His noble face is perishing and vain, and that: He will regard every deed they have done that was not for His noble face and make it scattered dust, and He will nullify what they produced and render void what they used to do; and that there is no persistence for anything except His noble face. Thus, what is done for His noble face and performed in His name is that which remains and does not vanish. Every matter among matters only possesses a share of persistence in proportion to the share Allah has in it. This is what is conveyed by what has been narrated by both groups [Shi'a and Sunni] from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) that he said: **كل امر ذي بال لم يبدأ فيه باسم الله فهو ابتر الحديث** — "Every matter of importance that is not begun with the name of Allah is severed (أبتر)." The "severed" (الأبتر) is that which is cut off at the end. Therefore, it is most appropriate that the referent of the preposition (متعلق الباء) in the *Basmalah* (البسملة) is "I begin" (أبتدئ) in the sense we have mentioned. He has begun the speech with it insofar as it is an act among acts, so it must possess a unity (وحدة), and the unity of speech is through the unity of its significance and meaning. Thus, it must have a meaning possessing unity, which is the meaning intended to be conveyed by the delivery of the speech and the goal achieved from it.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned the goal achieved from His speech, which is the entirety of the Quran, when the Exalted said: **قَدْ جَاءَكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَابٌ مُّبِينٌ يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ** — "Certainly there has come to you from Allah a light and a manifest Book. With it Allah guides..." (5:15-16), among other verses in which He stated: that the purpose of His Book and His speech is the guidance (الهداية) of the servants. Thus, guidance in its entirety is what begins with the name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. He is Allah (الله), to Whom is the return of the servants; He is the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), Who clarifies to His servants the path of His general mercy (رحمته العامة) encompassing the believer and the disbeliever, regarding that which contains their good in their existence and life; and He is the Most Merciful (الرحيم), Who clarifies to them the path of His special mercy (رحمته الخاصة) for the believers, which is the felicity of their hereafter and the meeting with their Lord. The Exalted has said: **وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ** — "...My mercy embraces all things, and I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious..." (7:156). This is in relation to the entirety of the Quran.

Furthermore, He, the Glorified, has frequently repeated the mention of the "Surah" (السورة) in His speech, such as the saying of the Exalted: **فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِ** — "...then bring a surah like it..." (10:38), and His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَيَاتٍ** — "...then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated..." (11:13), and the saying of the Exalted: **إِذَا أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ** — "Whenever a surah is sent down..." (9:86), and His saying: **سُورَةٌ أَنزَلْنَاهَا وَفَرَضْنَاهَا** — "[This is] a surah which We have sent down and which We have made obligatory..." (24:1). It becomes clear to us from this: that each of these groups of His speech (which He has divided into distinct sections and named each section a Surah) possesses a type of unity of composition and completeness that is not found between parts of a single Surah, nor between one Surah and another. From here we know: that the goals and purposes achieved from the Surahs are different, and that each one of them is directed toward the explanation of a specific meaning and a realized goal that the Surah does not complete except by its completion. Accordingly, the *Basmalah* at the beginning of each Surah refers back to the specific goal of that Surah.

Thus, the *Basmalah* in *Surat al-Hamd* refers back to the goal of the Surah and the meaning achieved from it. The goal indicated by the flow of speech in this Surah is the praise (الحمد) of Allah by manifesting servitude (العبودية) to Him, the Glorified, through the articulation of worship (العبادة), seeking assistance (الاستعانة), and asking for guidance (الهداية). It is speech that Allah, the Glorified, speaks on behalf of the servant, so that the servant may be disciplined in the station of manifesting servitude by that which Allah has disciplined him with.

The manifestation of servitude by the servant is the act with which the servant clothes himself, and the matter of importance (الأمر ذو البال) that he undertakes. Thus, the beginning with the name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful, refers back to it. The meaning is: "In Your name, I manifest servitude to You."

Therefore, the referent of the preposition (متعلق الباء) in the *Basmalah* of *al-Hamd* is "the beginning" (الابتداء), and what is intended by it is the completion of sincerity (الإخلاص) in the station of servitude through direct address (التخاطب). It is sometimes said that it is "seeking assistance" (الاستعانة), and there is no harm in that, but "the beginning" is more appropriate because the Surah explicitly includes seeking assistance in the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ** — "...and You [alone] do we beseech for help" (1:5).

As for the "name" (الاسم), it is the word (اللفظ) that denotes the named (المسمى). It is derived either from *al-simah* (السمة) meaning "the mark" (العلامة) or from *al-sumuww* (السمو) meaning "loftiness" (الرفعة). In any case, what is known of it in language and custom is the denoting word, and this necessitates that it be other than the named. As for the "name" in the sense of the Essence (الذات) taken with one of its attributes, it is among the external entities (الأعيان), not the words; it is the "named of the name" (مسمى الاسم) in the first sense. Just as the word "the All-Knowing" (العالم)—among the names of Allah, the Exalted—is a name denoting its named, which is the Essence taken with the attribute of knowledge; and it is itself a "name" in relation to the Essence, about which no report is given except by one of its attributes or one of its descriptions. The reason for this is that they found the word "name" (الاسم) coined for the word denoting the named; then they found that the attributes taken in a way that recounts the Essence and denotes it are like the word called "name" in that they denote external essences. So they called these attributes denoting the essences "names" as well. This resulted in the "name" being both a verbal matter (أمراً لفظياً) and an external/essential matter (أمراً عينياً). Then they found that the indicator of the Essence that is close to it is the "name" in the second sense reached through analysis, and that the "name" in the first sense only denotes the Essence through its mediation. For this reason, they called that which is in the second sense "the name" (إسماً), and that which is in the first sense "the name of the name" (اسم الاسم). However, all of this is a matter led to by theoretical analysis and should not be imposed upon the language; for the "name" according to the language is what we have mentioned.

A dispute became widespread among the theologians (المتكلمين) in the first period of Islam as to whether the name is identical to the named (عين المسمى) or other than it (غيره), and the arguments over it were prolonged. However, this type of issue has become so clear today that it reaches the level of necessity (الضرورة), and it is not permissible to be occupied with it by mentioning what has been said or is being said about it, or by being concerned with nullifying what is false and establishing what is true therein; thus, turning away from it is preferable.

As for the "Majestic Word" (لفظ الجلالة), *Allah* (الله), its origin is *al-Ilah* (الإله). The *hamzah* was omitted due to frequent usage. *Ilah* is from *alaha al-rajul ya'lahu* (أله الرجل يأله) meaning "he worshipped," or from *alaha al-rajul* or *waliha al-rajul* (أله الرجل أو وله الرجل) meaning "he was bewildered." It is of the form *fi'al* (فعال) with a *kasrah* on the *fa*, carrying the meaning of the object (المفعول), like *kitab* (كتاب) meaning "that which is written" (المكتوب). He is named *Ilah* because He is worshipped (معبود) or because He is that in whose Essence the intellects are bewildered. It appears that it is a proper noun by predominance (علم بالغلبة). It was already in use and circulating on tongues before the revelation of the Quran; the pre-Islamic Arab knew it, as suggested by the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ** — "If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah’" (43:87), and the saying of the Exalted: **فَقَالُوا هَٰذَا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهَٰذَا لِشُرَكَائِنَا** — "...and they say, ‘This is for Allah,’ so they claim, ‘and this is for our partners’" (6:136).

Among the proofs of its being a proper noun (علم) is that it is described by all the Beautiful Names (الأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ) and His other acts derived from those names, without the reverse. It is said: "Allah is the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful," and it is said: "Allah showed mercy," "Allah knew," and "Allah provided." The Majestic Word does not occur as an attribute for any of them, nor is anything derived from it to describe any of them.

Since His existence, the Glorified—and He is the God (إله) of everything—leads to His being characterized by all the attributes of perfection (الصفات الكمالية), they were all denoted by it by implication (بالالتزام). Thus, what has been said is correct: that the Majestic Word is a name for the Essence that is the Necessary Existent (الواجب الوجود) which encompasses all attributes of perfection; otherwise, it is a proper noun by predominance that has not been affected by any consideration other than what its root *i-l-h* (إله) denotes.

**As for the two attributes:** the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) and the Most Merciful (الرحيم), they are both from "mercy" (الرحمة). Mercy is an affective attribute (وصف انفعالي) and a specific impression that touches the heart upon witnessing one who lacks or needs that which completes his affair, which then impels the human being to complete his deficiency and remove his need. However, this meaning, according to analysis, refers back to "giving" (الإعطاء) and "bestowing" (الإفاضة) to remove the need; and in this sense, the Glorified is characterized by mercy.

*Al-Rahman* (الرحمن) is of the form *fa'lan* (فعلان), an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة) denoting abundance (الكثرة). *Al-Rahim* (الرحيم) is of the form *fa'il* (فعيل), a permanent attribute (صفة مشبهّة) denoting stability and persistence (الثبات والبقاء). Therefore, it was appropriate for *al-Rahman* to denote the abundant mercy bestowed upon the believer and the disbeliever, which is the general mercy (الرحمة العامة). In this sense, it is frequently used in the Quran; the Exalted said: **الرَّحْمَٰنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوَىٰ** — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne" (20:5), and He said: **قُلْ مَن كَانَ فِي الضَّلَالَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ الرَّحْمَٰنُ مَدًّا** — "Say, ‘Whoever is in error, the All-beneficent shall prolong his respite’" (19:75), among others. For this reason also, it was appropriate for *al-Rahim* to denote the permanent blessing (النِعمَةِ الدائمة) and the stable, persistent mercy (الرَّحمة الثَّابتَة الباقية) that is bestowed upon the believer, as the Exalted said: **وَكَانَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيمًا** — "...and He is most merciful to the faithful" (33:43), and the Exalted said: **إِنَّهُ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "...Indeed He is most kind and merciful to them" (9:117), among others. Therefore, it is said: that *al-Rahman* is general for the believer and the disbeliever, and *al-Rahim* is specific to the believer.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** الحمد لله (All praise belongs to Allah). Praise (الحمد), according to what has been said, is the commendation of a beautiful act performed voluntarily (الجميل الاختياري), while "laudation" (المدح) is more general than it. It is said: "I praised (*hamadtu*) so-and-so" or "I lauded (*madahtu*) him" for his generosity; and it is said: "I lauded (*madahtu*) the pearl" for its purity, but it is not said: "I praised (*hamadtu*) it" for its purity. The "L" (اللام) in it is for the genus (للجنس) or for exhaustiveness (الاستغراق), and the ultimate result here is the same.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, says: **ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "That is Allah, your Lord, the Creator of all things..." (40:62). Thus, He stated that whatever is a "thing" is a creation of Allah, the Glorified. And He said: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** — "...who perfected everything He created..." (32:7). Thus, He established beauty (الحسن) for every created thing from the aspect that it is His creation attributed to Him. So beauty revolves around creation and vice versa; there is no creation but that it is beautiful and good by His perfection, and there is no beauty but that it is His creation attributed to Him. The Exalted has said: **هُوَ اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** — "...He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (39:4). And He said: **وَعَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ** — "All faces shall be humbled before the Living One, the All-sustainer..." (20:111). Thus, He informed that He did not create what He created by the coercion of a subduer, nor does He do what He does by the compulsion of a compeller; rather, He created it out of knowledge and choice. So there is no thing but that it is a beautiful, voluntary act of His. This is from the aspect of the act. As for the aspect of the name, the Exalted has said: **اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** — "Allah—there is no god except Him—to Him belong the Best Names" (20:8). And the Exalted said: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمَائِهِ** — "To Allah belong the Best Names, so call Him by them, and leave those who blaspheme His names..." (7:180). Thus, He, the Exalted, is beautiful in His names and beautiful in His acts, and every beautiful thing is from Him.

It has thus become clear that He, the Exalted, is praised for His beautiful names and praised for His beautiful acts, and that there is no praise that a praiser offers for a praised matter except that it belongs to Allah, the Glorified, in reality, because the beauty to which the praise relates is from Him, the Glorified. Thus, to Allah, the Glorified, belongs the genus of praise, and to Him, the Glorified, belongs every praise.

Furthermore, it is apparent from the context and by the evidence of the "shift in address" (الالتفات) in His saying: **إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ** — "You [alone] do we worship..." (1:5), that the Surah is from the speech of the servant, and that He, the Glorified, in this Surah, dictates to His servant the praise of Himself and the etiquette that the servant should observe when placing himself in the station of servitude. This is what is supported by His saying: **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** — "All praise belongs to Allah."

This is because praise is a description (توصيف), and He, the Glorified, has sanctified Himself from the description of the describers among His servants where He said: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "Clear is Allah of what they allege [about Him]—[all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160). The speech is absolute and unrestricted, and there has not appeared in His speech, the Exalted, anything that permits the recounting of praise from other than Him except what He recounted from a number of His chosen prophets. The Exalted said in His address to Noah (peace be upon him): **فَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي نَجَّانَا مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "...say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has delivered us from the wrongdoing lot’" (23:28). And the Exalted said, recounting from Abraham (peace be upon him): **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ** — "All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age..." (14:39). And the Exalted said to His Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in several places of His speech: **وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** — "And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah...’" (27:93). And the Exalted said, recounting from David and Solomon (peace be upon them): **وَقَالَا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** — "...and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah...’" (27:15). Except also what He recounted from the people of Paradise, and they are the ones purified from rancor in the breasts, vain talk, and sinfulness, such as His saying: **وَآخِرُ دَعْوَاهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "...and their concluding call is, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (10:10).

As for other than these instances, although He, the Exalted, recounts praise from many of His creation—rather, from all of them—such as the saying of the Exalted: **وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ** — "...and the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord..." (42:5), and His saying: **وَيُسَبِّحُ الرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِ** — "The thunder celebrates His praise..." (13:13), and His saying: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ** — "...and there is not a thing but celebrates His praise..." (17:44); yet He, the Glorified, coupled the praise in all of them with glorification (التسبيح). Rather, He made glorification the primary element in the recounting and placed praise with it. This is because other than Him, the Exalted, cannot encompass the beauty of His acts and their perfection, just as they cannot encompass the beauty of His attributes and names, from which the beauty of the acts stems. The Exalted said: **وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْمًا** — "...while they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge" (20:110). Thus, whatever they describe Him with, they have encompassed it, and it has become limited by their limits and measured by the extent of their reach of Him. So the commendation they offer does not stand correct except after they sanctify Him and glorify Him from what they have limited and measured with their understandings. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** — "...Indeed Allah knows and you do not know" (16:74). As for the chosen servants (المخلصون) among His servants, He has made their praise His praise and their description His description, since He made them chosen for Himself. It has thus become clear that what the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) requires is that the servant praise his Lord with that which He has praised Himself, and not exceed it, as in the hadith narrated by both groups from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): **لا أحصي ثناء عليك أنت كما أثنيت على نفسك الحديث** — "I cannot enumerate Your praise; You are as You have praised Yourself." Thus, His saying at the beginning of this Surah: "All praise belongs to Allah," is a disciplining with a servant-like etiquette that the servant would not have been able to say had Allah, the Exalted, not said it by way of representation and teaching of how the commendation should be.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** رب العالمين الرحمن الرحيم مالك يوم الدين (Lord of all the worlds, the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful, Master of the Day of Retribution) etc. (and the majority read *Malik* [King] of the Day of Retribution). The "Lord" (الرب) is the owner (المالك) who manages the affairs of his possession; thus, it contains the meaning of ownership (الملك) and the meaning of management. Ownership (الملك)—that which we have in the social sphere—is a specific type of exclusive relationship (اختصاص), which is a type of subsistence of one thing by another that necessitates the validity of disposals (التصرفات) over it. So our saying "such-and-such an object is our property" means: that it has a type of subsistence and exclusive relationship with us by which our disposals over it are valid, and without that, those disposals would not be valid. This, in society, is a conventional, relative meaning (معنى وضعيّ اعتباريّ), not a real one. It is derived from another real meaning which we also call ownership, which is the way the parts of our existence and our powers subsist by us; for we have sight, hearing, a hand, and a foot. The meaning of this ownership is that in their existence they are subsistent by our existence, not independent of us, but rather independent through our independence, and we may dispose of them as we wish. This is the real ownership (الملك الحقيقي).

That which can be attributed to Him, the Exalted, according to reality is the reality of ownership, not the relative ownership that vanishes with the vanishing of convention and status. It is well known that real ownership is inseparable from management (التدبير); for if a thing is in need of something for its existence and is not independent of it in its existence, it is not independent of it in the effects of its existence. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of whatever is other than Him, because the Lord is the owner-manager (المالك المدبّر), and He, the Exalted, is such.

**As for "all the worlds" (العالمين):** it is the plural of *al-'alam* (العالم) with a *fathah* on the *lam*, meaning "that by which one knows," like *al-qalab* (the mold), *al-khatam* (the seal), and *al-tabi'* (the stamp), meaning that by which one molds, seals, and stamps. It is applied to all existents and to every species composed of individuals and parts, such as the world of minerals, the world of plants, the world of animals, and the world of humans. It is also applied to every category whose individuals are gathered together, such as the world of the Arabs and the world of the non-Arabs. This meaning is the most appropriate for what the enumeration of these Beautiful Names leads to, until it reaches His saying: "Master of the Day of Retribution," on the basis that the "Retribution" (الدين), which is the recompense on the Day of Resurrection, is specific to humans or humans and jinn. Thus, the intended meaning of "all the worlds" would be the worlds of humans and jinn and their communities. This is supported by the occurrence of this word with this consideration in the Quran, such as the saying of the Exalted: **وَاصْطَفَاكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَاءِ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "...and chosen you above the women of all the worlds" (3:42), and the saying of the Exalted: **لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَالَمِينَ نَذِيرًا** — "...that he may be a warner to all the worlds" (25:1), and the saying of the Exalted: **أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "Do you commit this indecency, the like of which no one in all the worlds has ever committed before you?" (7:80).

**As for "Master of the Day of Retribution" (مالك يوم الدين):** you have already learned the meaning of "Master" (*Malik*), which is derived from *milk* (ownership) with a *kasrah* on the *mim*. As for "King" (*Malik*), which is derived from *mulk* (sovereignty) with a *dammah* on the *mim*, he is the one who possesses the national system and their management, rather than the physical entity; in other words, he possesses the authority (الأمر) and the judgment (الحكم) over them.

Arguments of support have been mentioned for each of the two readings, *Malik* (King) and *Maalik* (Master); however, both meanings of dominion (السلطنة) are established for Him, the Exalted. What language and custom recognize is that *Mulk* (sovereignty) with a *dammah* on the *mim* is that which is attributed to time; it is said: "the King (*Malik*) of such-and-such an era," and it is not said "the Master (*Maalik*) of such-and-such an era" except with a remote consideration. The Exalted has said: "King (*Malik*) of the Day of Retribution," thus attributing it to the Day. He also said: **لِّمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — "...‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Uyun* and *al-Ma'ani*, narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of His saying: بسم الله (In the Name of Allah). He (peace be upon him) said: "It means I mark my soul with one of the marks (السمات) of Allah, which is worship (العبادة)." He was asked: "What is the mark (السمة)?" He replied: "The sign (العلامة)."

**I say:** This meaning is as if born from the meaning we pointed to regarding the "B" (الباء) being for the beginning; for if the servant marks his worship with the name of Allah, it necessitates that he marks his soul—to which he attributes the worship—with one of His marks.

In *al-Tahdhib* from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and in *al-Uyun* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "It is closer to the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الاعظم) than the pupil of the eye is to its white."

**I say:** The meaning of the narration will come in the discussion on the Greatest Name.

In *al-Uyun* from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "It is from the *Fatihah*, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) used to recite it and count it as a verse from it, and he would say: 'The Opening of the Book is the Seven Oft-Repeated [Verses] (السبع المثاني).'"

**I say:** A similar meaning has been narrated through the paths of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah*. From al-Daraqutni from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'When you recite *al-Hamd*, then recite *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim*, for it is the Mother of the Quran (امّ القرآن) and the Seven Oft-Repeated [Verses], and *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim* is one of its verses.'"

In *al-Khisal* from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "What is the matter with them? May Allah fight them! They aimed at the greatest verse in the Book of Allah and claimed it is an innovation (بدعة) if they manifest it."

From al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "They stole the most noble verse in the Book of Allah: *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim*. It should be performed at the opening of every matter, great or small, so that it may be blessed."

**I say:** The narrations from the Imams of the *Ahl al-Bayt* regarding this meaning are many, and they all indicate that the *Basmalah* is a part of every Surah except *Surat al-Bara'ah* [al-Tawbah]. Among the narrations of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah* are those that indicate this.

In *Sahih Muslim* from Anas: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'A Surah was just revealed to me,' and he recited: *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim*."

From Abu Dawud from Ibn Abbas (and they have authenticated its chain): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) did not know the separation of the Surah (and in a narration: the conclusion of the Surah) until *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim* was revealed to him."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through the paths of the elite (الخاصة) from al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kafi*, *al-Tawhid*, *al-Ma'ani*, and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "Allah is the God of everything, the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) to all His creation, the Most Merciful (الرحيم) to the believers specifically."

It is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "*Al-Rahman* is a specific name with a general attribute, and *al-Rahim* is a general name with a specific attribute."

**I say:** It has become clear from what has passed the reason for the generality of *al-Rahman* for the believer and the disbeliever, and the specificity of *al-Rahim* for the believer. As for *al-Rahman* being a specific name with a general attribute and *al-Rahim* being a general name with a specific attribute, it is as if he means by it that *al-Rahman* is specific to this world and encompasses the disbeliever and the believer, while *al-Rahim* is general for this world and the hereafter and is specific to the believers. In other words: *al-Rahman* is specific to the existential bestowal (الافاضة التكوينية) which encompasses the believer and the disbeliever, while *al-Rahim* encompasses the existential and the legislative (التشريع)—the door of which is the door of guidance and felicity—and is specific to the believers because stability and persistence are specific to the blessings bestowed upon them, and the [good] end is for godwariness (التقوی).

In *Kashf al-Ghummah* from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "My father (peace be upon him) lost a mule and said: 'If Allah returns it to me, I shall surely praise Him with praises that please Him.' It was not long before it was brought with its saddle and bridle. When he settled [upon it] and gathered his clothes to him, he raised his head to the sky and said: 'All praise belongs to Allah' (الحمد لله), and did not add more. Then he said: 'I have not left out nor omitted anything; I have placed all types of praises for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, for there is no praise but that it is included therein.'"

I say: In *al-Uyun* from Ali (peace be upon him), he was asked about its interpretation and said: "It is that Allah has made His servants recognize some of His blessings upon them in general, since they are unable to recognize all of them in detail because they are more than can be counted or known. So He said: 'Say: All praise belongs to Allah for what He has blessed us with.'"

**I say:** He (peace be upon him) is pointing to what has passed: that the praise is from the servant, and Allah only mentioned it by way of representation as a disciplining and teaching.

## (Philosophical Discussion)

Rational proofs establish that the independence of the effect (المعلول) and every one of its states is only through the cause (العلة), and that every perfection it possesses is from the shadows of the existence of its cause. Thus, if beauty and goodness have a reality in existence, then their perfection and independence belong to the Necessary [Existent], the Exalted, because He is the Cause to Whom all causes lead. Commendation and praise are the manifestation of an existent's perfection through its existence for another existent, which is inevitably its cause. Since every perfection leads to Him, the Exalted, the reality of every commendation and praise returns and leads to Him, the Exalted. Thus: "All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds."

**His saying, the Exalted:** إياك نعبد وإياك نستعين (You [alone] do we worship, and You [alone] do we beseech for help) etc. The "servant" (العبد) is the human being who is owned, or every conscious being by abstracting the meaning, as suggested by the saying of the Exalted: **إِن كُلُّ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمَٰنِ عَبْدًا** — "There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-beneficent as a servant" (19:93). Worship (العبادة) is derived from it, and its derivations or the meanings in which it is used may differ due to the difference of instances. What al-Jawhari mentioned in *al-Sihah*—that the root of servitude (العبودية) is "submission" (الخضوع)—is from the aspect of taking the necessary implication of the meaning; otherwise, "submission" is transitive with the preposition *lam*, while "worship" is transitive by itself.

In short, it is as if worship is the servant's placing of himself in the station of being owned by his Lord. For this reason, worship was incompatible with arrogance (الاستكبار) but not incompatible with association (الاشتراك). It is permissible for more than one to share in the ownership of a person or in the worship of a servant. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ** — "...Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me shall enter hell, abased" (40:60). And the Exalted said: **وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَدًا** — "...and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord" (18:110). Thus, He considered association possible, and for that reason He forbade it; and prohibition can only be regarding that which is possible and within power, unlike arrogance toward worship, for it does not coexist with it.

Servitude (العبودية) only stands correct between servants and their masters in that which the masters own of them. As for that which ownership does not relate to among the states of the servant's existence—such as his being the son of so-and-so or his being tall in stature—worship and servitude do not relate to it. However, Allah, the Glorified, in His ownership of His servants, is contrary to this description. Neither is His ownership mixed with the ownership of anyone else, nor is the servant divided in his relationship to Him, the Exalted, such that one part of him is owned and another part is not owned. Nor is one disposal among disposals over him permissible and another disposal impermissible. This is unlike servants among us, where something of them is owned—which is their voluntary acts—and something is not owned—which is their involuntary attributes; and some disposals over them are permissible—such as benefiting from their acts—and some are not permissible—such as killing them without a crime, for example. He, the Exalted, is the Owner in an absolute sense (على الاطلاق) without condition or restriction, and other than Him is owned in an absolute sense without condition or restriction. Thus, there is a restriction (حصر) from two sides: the Lord is confined to ownership, and the servant is confined to servitude. This is what is indicated by His saying: "You [alone] do we worship," where the object was placed first and worship was mentioned without restriction.

Furthermore, since ownership is that whose existence is constituted by its owner—as you have learned from what has passed—it does not act as a veil from its owner, nor is it veiled from him. For if you look at the house of Zayd, if you look at it from the aspect that it is a house, it is possible for you to be heedless of Zayd; but if you look at it insofar as it is the property of Zayd, it is not possible for you to be heedless of its owner, who is Zayd.

But you have learned that whatever is other than Him, the Exalted, possesses nothing but being owned only, and this is its reality. Thus, nothing of it, in reality, is veiled from Him, the Exalted; nor does looking at it coexist with heedlessness of Him, the Exalted. Therefore, He, the Exalted, possesses absolute presence (الحضور المطلق). The Glorified said: **أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ أَلَا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَاءَ رَبِّهِمْ أَلَا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُّحِيطٌ** — "...Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? Look! They are indeed in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Look! He indeed encompasses all things!" (41:53-54). If it is so, then the right of His worship, the Exalted, is that it be from a presence on both sides.

As for the side of the Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, it is that He be worshipped with the worship of a present deity, which is what necessitates the shift (التفات)—taken in the saying of the Exalted: "You [alone] do we worship"—from absence (الغيبة) to presence (الحضور).

As for the side of the servant, it is that his worship be the worship of a present servant, without being absent in his worship such that his worship becomes a form only without meaning and a body without spirit; or that he be divided, occupying himself with his Lord and with other than Him—either outwardly and inwardly, like the idolaters in their worship of Allah and their idols together, or inwardly only, like one who occupies himself in his worship with other than Him, the Exalted, in the manner of goals and purposes; such as worshipping Allah while his concern is in other than Him, or worshipping Allah out of greed for Paradise or fear of the Fire. All of that is from the association in worship (الشرك في العبادة) regarding which prohibition has been issued. The Exalted said: **فَاعْبُدِ اللَّهَ مُخْلِصًا لَّهُ الدِّينَ** — "...So worship Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him" (39:2). And the Exalted said: **أَلَا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخَالِصُ** — "Look! [Only] exclusive faith is worthy of Allah..." (39:3).

Thus, worship is only true worship if it is based on sincerity (خلوص) from the servant, which is the presence we have mentioned. It has become clear that it is only completed if he does not occupy himself with other than Him, the Exalted, in his act—for then he would have given him partnership with Allah, the Glorified, in his worship—and if his heart does not attach itself in his worship to a hope or fear that is the ultimate goal in his worship, such as Paradise or the Fire—for then his worship would be for it, not for the face of Allah—and if he does not occupy himself with his own self—for that would be incompatible with the station of servitude, which does not harmonize with egoism (الإنيّة) and arrogance. It is as if the use of the first-person plural (المتكلم مع الغير) is to hint at this point; for in it is a humbling of the self by nullifying its specific determination and its individual standing alone, which necessitates a type of egoism and independence, unlike placing it within the group and mixing it with the mass of people, for in that is the erasure of determination and the effacement of the trace, whereby that [egoism] is secured against.

From all of that, it has become clear: that the manifestation of servitude by the saying: "You [alone] do we worship," does not contain any deficiency in terms of meaning and in terms of sincerity, except what is in the saying: "You [alone] do we worship" of the servant's attribution of worship to himself, which necessarily includes a claim of independence in existence, power, and will, even though he is owned, and the owned owns nothing. So it is as if that was rectified by the saying of the Exalted: "and You [alone] do we beseech for help" (وإياك نستعين); meaning, we only attribute worship to ourselves and claim it for us while seeking assistance from You, not as independent ones claiming it apart from You. Thus, His saying: "You [alone] do we worship, and You [alone] do we beseech for help," is to express a single meaning, which is worship out of sincerity. It is possible that this is the reason for the union of seeking assistance and worship in the context of address, where it was said: "You [alone] do we worship, and You [alone] do we beseech for help," without saying: "You [alone] do we worship, [so] help us and guide us to the straight path."

As for the change of context in His saying: "Guide us to the [straight] path..." etc., the discussion on it will come, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

It has become clear by what has passed of the explanation of His saying: "You [alone] do we worship, and You [alone] do we beseech for help," the reason for the shift from absence to presence, the reason for the restriction (الحصر) conveyed by the preceding object, the reason for the unrestricted use of His saying: "we worship," the reason for choosing the first-person plural, the reason for following the first sentence with the second, and the reason for the partnership of the two sentences in the context. The commentators have mentioned other points regarding these matters; whoever desires them should refer to their books. And He, Allah, the Glorified, is a creditor (غريم) whose debt is never fully paid.
